data.text:
INT: Tape two of Mark Verstandig-- please continue your story from the time your father built the house in Mielec. 

MV: Yes, maybe a year later or so, he bought-- he bought a large estate called Sadkowa Gora, 17 kilometers out of-- from Mielec. As a matter of fact, a broker took him over there, [NON-ENGLISH], and he came back and told my wife-- told his wife, I just bought a real-- I just bought an estate-- [NON-ENGLISH]. 

It was interesting that this estate, when it came to transfer the-- came to transfer the title, it came out that there was, on the title, a clause that this estate should never be sold to a Jew. So my father was worried. He went to this-- to the solicitor, a certain Dr. Eisenberg-- was a young man, a solicitor from Vienna. He was a Galiciana, but he studied in Vienna. 

And I mention his name because he-- in-- I mean, later I'll be talking about him, too, during the war and before the war. So this Dr. Eisenberg said, you shouldn't worry. I will fix it. This clause is contra bonos mores. It's against good behavior. And under-- under the OZN Constitution, it is invalid. It must have been-- it must have come at a time when Jews, after the emancipation, were allowed to buy and lend. 

And then, an antisemite was the owner, put it into-- made the clause that this should never be sold to a Jew. But it is not valid anymore according to the OZN Constitution, and it will be transferred. And this is what happened-- it was transferred to my father. 

You will see later, my-- my daughter brought you-- brought these pictures of the manor, of the house, of the manor, which she visited-- which she visited when she was in Poland a few years ago. 

And so he became a landowner. He knew farming-- because his father was a farmer, so he knew farming, and he liked farming. So really, farming was his profession. And besides this, he made money in Sweden. 

And this was going on until the First World War. After-- after the First World War, he was still farming, he was farming, and he had other businesses, just the same. So he had a wholesale business selling spirits-- 96-proof spirits, in big barrels. And from these big barrels, the wholesale-- wholesale to-- to the wholesale business. They used to sell to shops. Shops used to come to buy spirits, to buy vodka. 

That was-- for that purpose, one must have had a license. How he got his license, I don't know. But he had. It was a license-- it was a licensed business, a profitable one, because it was a monopoly. And then he had the monopoly selling tobacco. I don't know how he got this license. 

And in this house, which he built, on the ground floor were two shops. One shop was a-- a retail tobacco shop. And the other was a clothing shop. The clothing shop was in the hands of my aunt, Vocha Ostrow-- Vocha and Abraham Ostrow. 

And the tobacco was being-- tobacco cigarettes were-- were sold by a Polish invalid after the First World War, after the First World War. It was a Polish invalid who had a license. And that was a gold mine. 

The peasants used to come to town, and there used to be not queues, but crowds of people trying to get into this shop. Not-- I'm not telling you joking. There were crowds of people pushing themselves into this shop. It was a very good shop. 

Now, one day, one day he died. And his widow came to my father. And she wanted to sell the license. Anyhow, my father paid her some money and took over this shop-- a retail shop. At that time, he was a rash hakahal. That means the president of the Jewish community. 

The office of the Jewish community was on the first floor of our house, it was a large house. There were some two rooms for his office. And when there was a meeting, the meeting used to be in the dining room, our dining room, the meetings of the councilors, of the Jewish councilors. 

And downstairs, I mean, on the-- on the ground floor was the shop. And he thought that he can run the shop. Now, he didn't have a clue how to run a shop, how to run business, how to run a retail business whatsoever. And after a short time, he had to get-- yeah, besides this, he employed-- he employed an invalid, again. He employed one of these invalids, in order-- in order to validate the transfer of license. With the license, he had to make some agreements with the union of the invalids. 

And so he put in somebody there. Anyhow, after a quite, maybe a-- a couple of-- a couple of years, three years, and so on-- a few years, this gold mine turned into-- into a-- into a pit-- into a pit of losses. He-- he had no idea how to run a shop, and he gave it away. He gave it away to the invalids. 

And that was the business of my father. He was-- he was a landowner, a farmer, and he never-- he never had any trouble with money. But then, about '20-- maybe '24, '25, 1924, 1924-- you see, my brother had to-- he was-- was 20 years-- '24, 21 years old, and he was conscripted. And that was a catastrophe. 

Normally, Jewish sons tried not to get into the army, because the army was antisemitic. The officers, and the-- the sergeants, the corporals, they all were-- very antisemitic. And besides this, there was the dietary laws were-- were not in-- were not in conformity with Jewish law. You see, you couldn't-- you couldn't eat kosher. 

And my father and my mother were orthodox people. And so were many, many-- most of the Jews. So normally, they tried to get out of the army. 

At about the time when the commission deciding on conscription was sitting for a few weeks in town. And all people reaching 21 years had to-- had to undergo an inspection, it they were able to go to the army. 

Now, people used to walk around for nights for three months, for four months, trying to get skinny, look sickly. And before standing with the-- before appearing in the-- and a bit-- a few hours, an hour or so before appearing before the commission, they were coming into-- into this room. They used to drink strong coffee-- a coffee with, let us say, 20 times the strength of a normal coffee. So really, you should force yourself to drink a cup of coffee like that. 

But after you drink a few cups of coffee, your heart was beating like you would have-- like you would have run kilometers. So when they came-- when they came to the committee, when they stood before this doctor, you see, their heart was abnormally beating there. But it helped when the doctor got $200 US-- a bribe. And that was a hell of a lot of money. With $200, you could make a living in Poland at that time. 

And see, because the time of inflation, and $200 used to give you 10% per month-- per month-- interest. So with $200, one could make a living out of interest. But with a large amount of money, only rich people, wealthy people, could afford it. 

And so when the doctor got $200, and the heart of the young-- of the young man that was beating, you see, he was freed from-- he was freed from-- from this-- from military service. But military service was really a catastrophe. They were beating up the Jews. And besides this, as I said told you there, it's not so far, but the-- the officer corps was antisemitic. And the other peasants who came to the army were under the influence of the sergeant, of the corporal, and so on. 

And this was the reason that people tried to get out of it. 

INT: How long did it last? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: How long did military service last? 

MV: Two years. It was two years. Now, if can-- it came the time my brother had to-- had to stand before the commission. He was 21 years, he was born in 1903, it was 1924. It was a dilemma, because my father was sitting, was a member of this commission. Not only my father, but when people used to come, let us say, from a-- from a little village called Borowa, the mayor of Borowa or the Soltis used to sit there to identify the people. Otherwise, somebody else could have come. 

There was no-- people-- people didn't have IDs with photographs, like-- like they've got here. So there always was a representative of the community sitting as a-- as a member of this commission. So my father was a member of the commission when Mielec-- when the youngsters of Mielec had to stand before the commission. 

So my father was in a dilemma. Because if my-- if my brother, if his son would be freed from military service, the people would say that the rash hakahal, the president of the Jewish community, fixed it for his son, where others had to go-- poor people had to go to-- to be conscripted. Therefore, my father decided that the best would be to take my brother to a town called Sokolow, because he was born in Sokolow, '93, just before my father moved from Sokolow to Mielec. 

So it was the best-- it was the best he could have-- he could have invented-- an idea that he could take him to Sokolow. And there, the president of the community, the Jewish community in Sokolow was very friendly. Because my father, after the First World War, rebuilt a synagogue in Sokolow. 

The synagogue in Sokolow was burned down during the war. Then when my elder sister, Grincha, came back from Sweden, she is-- she lived in Sweden during the First World War, because it so happens that she went to Sweden, and didn't go back when the war broke out. So she stayed over there, and attended over there-- how it-- attended high school in Sweden. 

So when she came back, she became very ill, very sick. So when she became very sick, my-- my mother decided that she was-- she was making the decisions-- that my father should go to a miracle maker, to a great rabbi in another town, and give him some money that he should pray to the Lord in heaven that she should be-- that she should get out of this sickness. 

And my father went over there and told the rabbi that his daughter is very sick. And gave him an envelope containing some nice bank notes. And the rabbi looked up to heaven and said to him, don't worry, I've got a message for you. Your daughter, Grina, the daughter of Gela and Lajzer, will be healthy, will become healthy, will get out of it. But you have to rebuild the synagogue in Sokolow. The synagogue was burned down. 

So he built the synagogue. So he knew when he'd come to that-- that the president of the community will be very friendly, and will-- he will be helpful. Besides his $200 was-- were prepared to pay it for the doctor. That was the standard bribe. $200 US were prepared. 

In the meantime, it so happened, unfortunately, that when the date-- the day came for my brother to appear before the commission, the doctor became ill, and another one, a stand-in came along. He didn't get any-- any-- any bribe. So he asked-- and my brother didn't have any-- didn't have any coffees, strong coffees before that. 

So he said, this man is all right for the army. So he got a-- a so-called A, which means he has to go to the army. There was nothing what could have been done until he enrolled-- until he was-- until he enrolled to the army. 

But then an offensive started, and bribes were given right and left. And my father didn't have the money, so he had to borrow the money. And that was a time, as I told you, that if you borrowed $100, you had to pay 10% interest per month. And after-- after a few months, anyhow, the commission decided-- the commission, they took him to a hospital, and the commission decided that he is a cripple, that he is not good for anything. 

So they sent him out. But it cost a few thousand dollars. And that was a hell of a lot of money at that time. And that was the first dent in his finances. 

And then came 1929, the Great Depression. In the United States, shareholders threw themselves down from-- from sky-- from-- from the 100-- from the floor-- from the-- from the exchange, from the-- from the stock exchange, or-- or from their own houses. 

In Poland, there were no great shareholders, but there were landowners. And it affects the landowner to such an extent that they became almost bankrupt. Really, it didn't pay to produce. Put it that way. Cart of potatoes, potatoes-- we used to sell potatoes to distillers to make vodka. 100 kilo of potatoes were worth $2, 2 zlotys-- 2 zlotys, which is $0.40. 100 kilo potatoes. It cost more to produce them. It cost more to deliver them. 

So in the end, it came, it was a very, very bad time. And that was my time. That was my time. There was not a penny in the house, not a penny in the house. Because on top of it, a few years earlier-- I think it was in 1926 or '27, '27-- my father decided to improve the estate-- buy new machinery, erect new buildings, new sheds, new buildings, and so on-- especially machinery, agricultural machinery. 

And he got a loan of 100,000 zlotys from the rural bank. That was approximately according to the valuation, 10% of the value of the estate. There was no problem. It was four and a-- it was 4 and 1/2% because they didn't make-- by the way, they didn't make any differentiation between a Jewish landowner and a non-Jewish landowner. Landowners used to get these privileged, these privileged loans for improvements on 4 and 1/2%. This was a gift. 

And later, it was-- just later means two years later, two years later, the whole crop, everything together, didn't suffice to pay the interest in the bank. 4 and 1/2%, and it was not. 

My father was lucky in this respect that before the First World War, when some Poles used to emigrate to the United States, and they have very small holdings-- let's say, an acre of land, two acres of land-- next to our estate, he used to buy it from them. Because they didn't need it anymore, they went to the United States. 

So these little holdings were not encumbered with-- with the loan, with-- with the loan to-- from the-- the rural bank, were not encumbered to the rural bank. So he used to sell yearly, all those small holdings in order to pay the interest. And even that did not suffice, he had to borrow money. 

There was not a penny in the house. And that was when I-- when I was in the high school already, before matriculation. 

INT: Can we go back now to the beginning of your childhood? You mentioned earlier, your parents were orthodox. Was that the main tradition amongst the Jewish people in that area? 

MV: Yeah. The main tradition-- everybody, almost everybody was an-- was orthodox. There were a few Jews here and there who were not orthodox. For instance, there was a new-- that Eisenberg, the-- the barrister, solicitor and barrister who came from Vienna. And what we knew is, he is not orthodox. He used to say that he is eating matzo with ham. But he was still eating matzo. He didn't go to the synagogue. 

But normally, the-- even the-- the assimilated Jews used-- used to go still to the synagogue on Yom Kippur. And there were no so-called professional [INAUDIBLE]. But the mainstream was orthodox. And my father was orthodox. And my mother was ultra orthodox. So she-- that was an obsession. The kashruth was, for her, an obsession. 

She used to-- you see, we had-- yes, I told you, we had water running. So the girls, the domestics, we had two domestics always, a Polish, a Christian domestic, and a Jewish cook. And they-- my mother forced them to wash their hands so many times, because from [YIDDISH], from-- from-- from meat, from meat products, to milk products, you touch that, you have to wash your hands. They had swole hands-- they had swole hands from the cold water running, because the water was not heated. It was from-- from the cold water. 

And the cutlery and crockery for Pesach, for instance, was packed in another room on the 3rd floor. There was a kitchen, and we called it the Pesach kitchen. Not that we used it for Pesach-- we used it once a year, once a year, when we had this goose fat to prepare, to prepare the fat for Pesach. Though that was being done in Hanukkah, in Hanukkah. And later, when we had these [YIDDISH], these pieces of fat, after the fat was rendered from it. That was really a treat. 

Many people-- people used to come there for that-- children from all around, from-- from-- from the neighborhood. And now, she was very, very, very kosher. In-- in Sadkowa Gora, for instance, in our estate, we had two different kitchens. One kitchen for milk products and one kitchen for-- and one kitchen for meat products. They were not mixed. You can't mix, you see? 

This was-- and I used to tell her, I used to tell her when she asked, when she could see that I am not orthodox when I grew up and I'm not orthodox, I used to tell her, look. I think the Lord in heaven sent down-- the Lord in heaven sent down a quota of orthodoxy to-- per family, and you took all-- you-- you filled out all the-- all the quotas. Nothing was left for me. 

INT: Thank you, we'll continue next tape.
